coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive ratiometric fluorescence detection of dibutyl phthalate in liquor and water using bio-based fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymers

coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymer (DBP-FMIPs) was designed and prepared for the selective detection of dibutyl phthalate (DBP) in food samples. This was achieved using inclusion complexes formed between short amylose and DBP as precursors, with tetrafluoroterephthalonitrile, which possesses an electron-donor-acceptor type dipolar structure within a compact benzene backbone, serving as a crosslinking agent and fluorescent readout signal. DBP-FMIPs exhibit excellent fluorescence stability and high selectivity, with a response time of less than 3 min for DBP. Based on the blue-green fluorescence emitted by DBP-FMIPs (λem = 500 nm), this material provided the response signal, while the red-emitting carbon dots(R-CDs, λem = 680 nm) were used as an internal reference, constructing a ratiometric fluorescence probe (R-CDs/DBP-FMIPs). The fluorescence intensity ratio (I500/I680)0/(I500/I680) exhibited a linear response to DBP within a concentration range of 0.020–20 mg L−1, with a detection limit as low as 4.5 μg L−1, and its fluorescence color shifted from blue to red. The fluorescent probe was successfully applied for detecting DBP in liquor and drinking water samples, achieving recoveries of 88–107 % and a relative standard deviation of 1.1–6.4 %. This preparation method can also be adapted for synthesizing FMIPs targeting other hydrophobic compounds. Additionally, the developed ratiometric fluorescence probe shows great potential for the selective and visual detection of phthalates in complex samples.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive ratiometric fluorescence detection of dibutyl phthalate in liquor and water using bio-based fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymers

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymer (DBP-FMIPs) was designed and prepared for the selective detection of dibutyl phthalate (DBP) in food samples. This was achieved using inclusion complexes formed between short amylose and DBP as precursors, with tetrafluoroterephthalonitrile, which possesses an electron-donor-acceptor type dipolar structure within a compact benzene backbone, serving as a crosslinking agent and fluorescent readout signal. DBP-FMIPs exhibit excellent fluorescence stability and high selectivity, with a response time of less than 3 min for DBP. Based on the blue-green fluorescence emitted by DBP-FMIPs (λem = 500 nm), this material provided the response signal, while the red-emitting carbon dots(R-CDs, λem = 680 nm) were used as an internal reference, constructing a ratiometric fluorescence probe (R-CDs/DBP-FMIPs). The fluorescence intensity ratio (I500/I680)0/(I500/I680) exhibited a linear response to DBP within a concentration range of 0.020–20 mg L−1, with a detection limit as low as 4.5 μg L−1, and its fluorescence color shifted from blue to red. The fluorescent probe was successfully applied for detecting DBP in liquor and drinking water samples, achieving recoveries of 88–107 % and a relative standard deviation of 1.1–6.4 %. This preparation method can also be adapted for synthesizing FMIPs targeting other hydrophobic compounds. Additionally, the developed ratiometric fluorescence probe shows great potential for the selective and visual detection of phthalates in complex samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Dibutyl phthalate (DBP) is a monomeric plasticizer widely used to improve the durability and flexibility of plastic food-packaging materials [1]. However, due to its lack of chemical binding with these materials, DBP can easily migrate into food, posing a risk of human exposure through ingestion [2]. DBP has been linked to cancer, malformations, and genetic mutations in organisms [3,4]. Recognized as a priority pollutant by the United States, European Union, and China, the latter stipulates that the DBP level in food products must not exceed 0.3 mg kg−1 [5]. Consequently, there is an urgent need to develop simple, fast, on-site, and cost-effective methods for detecting DBP.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are widely used for the adsorption and detection of DBP due to their simple preparation process, high selectivity, good chemical stability, and low cost. In preparing MIPs (DBP-MIPs) for DBP, methacrylic acid (MAA) is typically used as the functional monomer, while ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) serves as the crosslinker. This process takes advantage of the hydrogen bonding between DBP and MAA to form imprinting cavities and recognition sites, thereby enabling selective adsorption of DBP [6–8]. To improve the elution efficiency of template molecules and reduce the amount of eluent needed, researchers have explored using azobenzene derivatives as functional monomers to prepare photo-responsive MIPs [9,10].

However, most reagents used in these studies are not environmentally friendly. For example, MAA has acute toxicity and can cause severe irritation to the skin and eyes, as well as wheezing and difficulty breathing [11]. As global technology shifts toward environmental sustainability, there is a growing awareness of the potential environmental threats posed by traditional, unsustainable molecular imprinting technology (MIT), which cannot meet the requirements of green and sustainable development. In response, Arabi et al. [12,13] proposed fourteen principles for green MIT. These principles address aspects such as element choice, polymerization implementation, energy input, imprinting strategies, waste treatment, and recovery, as well as the impacts of these processes on operator health and the environment. Based on these principles, the development and use of renewable and non-toxic raw materials for MIP preparation have become a major focus in the research of green MIPs.

Although bio-based materials such as dopamine (DA) [14,15], chitosan [16], cyclodextrin [17], cellulose [18], and sodium alginate [19] have been applied in green MIT, research on their use in DBP-MIPs preparation remains limited. Yang et al. [20] utilized oxidative self-polymerization of dopamine to prepare a biomimetic PDA-MIP coating, which served as a platform for in-situ reduction and growth of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), achieving selective enrichment and detection of methyl phthalate. Jana et al. used bio-derived α-cyclodextrin as the functional monomer and tetrafluoroterephthalonitrile (TFT) as the crosslinker to prepare porous bio-based MIPs for the selective removal of DBP [21]. Recently, Ye et al. developed a method using low-toxicity diallyl phthalate (DAP) as a virtual template for DBP [22]. Under mild conditions, they employed low-toxicity gallic acid, hexanediamine, tannic acid, and bio-derived DA to prepare three mussel-inspired MIPs for the removal of phthalate compounds.

Fluorescence detection methods are known for their simplicity, speed, and high sensitivity [23,24], but they often lack selectivity when used alone. By incorporating fluorescent materials such as fluorescein, fluorescent dyes, or fluorescent nanoparticles into MIPs, the binding sites between target molecules and MIPs can be transformed into detectable fluorescent signals [25–27]. The resulting fluorescence MIPs (FMIPs) not only exhibit high sensitivity but also possess selectivity and anti-interference capability. For example, Yang et al. synthesized FMIPs for the selective detection of DBP in distilled spirit and tap water samples by utilizing Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots as fluorescent readout signals [28,29]. They employed surface MIT, employing sol-gel or precipitation polymerization methods, respectively. Both FMIPs exhibited specific recognition and sensitive detection abilities for DBP, with a linear range of 5.0–50 μmol L−1 and a detection limit ranging from 0.08 to 0.27 μmol L−1. Zhang et al. prepared FMIPs (CdTe@ZIF-67@MIPs) utilizing both CdTe-QDs and ZIF-67 as the nucleus, with the MIPs layer applied onto the surface of both CdTe-QDs and ZIF-67 through sol–gel polymerization. ZIF-67, boasting high specific surface areas, enhanced the fluorescence response sensitivity and response speed of the CdTe@ZIF-67@MIPs [30]. Successfully, the CdTe@ZIF-67@MIPs was employed to detect DBP in water, fish, milk, and juice samples. In order to enhance the sensitivity and improve the self-correction capabilities of fluorescence detection methods based on FMIPs, while avoiding interference from biological background and environmental factors, You et al. developed a tricolor ratiometric fluorescence sensor using CdSe/ZnS QDs with emission wavelengths of 450, 530, and 630 nm for the detection of DBP in fish and seawater [31]. The detection results were consistent with those obtained by gas chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry. The above FMIPs all utilize cadmium-containing semiconductor quantum dots as fluorescent readout signals, and the preparation process is relatively complex, which does not meet the green imprinting process of MIT proposed by Arabi et al [12,13].

Tetrafluoroterephthalonitrile (TFT), serving as a rigid crosslinking agent, was initially employed in the preparation of porous cyclodextrin polymer for the rapid removal of phenol micropollutants from aqueous systems [32]. Subsequently, Zhang et al. used TFT to crosslink (2-hydroxypropyl)-β-cyclodextrin, obtaining the hydrophilic fluorescent material for the turn-on fluorescence detection of captopril in urine samples [33], while Xu and coworkers discovered that TFT is an effective fluorescent probe for detecting biological thiol species [34].

Building upon the insights gleaned from the aforementioned researches, we have pioneered a new method for preparing FMIPs (DBP-FMIPs) with high selectivity for DBP. This innovative approach utilizes TFT as a fluorescent crosslinking agent and the helical complex formed between DBP and short amylose (SAM) as a precursor. The method does not require additional fluorescent materials. DBP-FMIPs exhibit significant selectivity for DBP, resulting in pronounced fluorescence quenching. To enhance detection sensitivity, we integrated DBP-FMIPs with red carbon dots as reference signals to fabricate a ratiometric fluorescence probe. This method enables highly sensitive and visually discernible detection of DBP in liquor and drinking water samples. Our approach presents a promising avenue for designing and fabricating FMIPs utilizing a fluorescent crosslinking agent and demonstrates exceptional versatility.

Dibutyl phthalate (DBP), diethylene glycol dicarboxylate (DGD), and TFT were procured from Shanghai Yien Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). O-phenylenediamine (OPD) was sourced from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Benzoic acid (BA) and potassium hydrogen phthalate (PHP) were obtained from the Beijing Chemical Plant (Beijing, China). Dimethyl phthalate (DMP) and dihexyl phthalate (DHP) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Corn starch was acquired from Jilin Province Ginseng Food Co., Ltd. (Jilin, China). N,N′-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China).

The fluorescence spectra were recorded using the F-2700 fluorescence spectrometer (HITACHI, Japan), while transient lifetime testing was performed using the FLS920 steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted using the ESCALAB 250Xi from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Fisher, USA), with Al Kα (1486.6 eV) as an X-ray source. UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained using the UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR 360, Nicolet, Madison, USA), scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU8000, Tokyo, Japan), X-ray diffractometry (XRD-6100, Shimadzu, Japan), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis (Micromeritics, USA), were employed for material characterization.
SAM and its inclusion complex with DBP (DBP-SAM) were initially synthesized, and comprehensive details regarding their preparation and characterization are provided in the supporting information.

The preparation details of DBP-FMIPs are as follows. Initially, DBP-SAM (1.0 g), TFT (400 mg), and K2CO3 (1.2 g) were combined in a flask, followed by the addition of 30 mL of DMF under a N2 atmosphere. The mixture was stirred and heated at 85 °C under reflux for 2 days. Afterward, the resulting suspension was cooled to room temperature and neutralized with 1 M hydrochloric acid until no CO2 bubbles were observed. Following centrifugation, the precipitate underwent stirring in water for 15 min, in DMF for 30 min, and in dichloromethane for 15 min to eliminate hydrochloric acid and TFT. Subsequently, the mixture was soxhleted for 24 h with methanol until no DBP could be detected. The obtained DBP-FMIPs were dried at 60 °C for 12 h. For comparison, non-imprinted polymers (DBP-FNIPs) were synthesized using the same procedure, with the substitution of DBP-SAM for SAM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: R- CDs were prepared according to a previously reported method [35]. Green tea powder (3.0 g) was dispersed in a 50 mL ethanol solution and sonicated at 25 °C for 60 min. After centrifugation of the mixture, the supernatant was filtered using a 0.45 μm cellulose membrane. The resulting R-CDs solution was stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding experiment of DBP-FMIPs and DBP-FNIPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The isothermal adsorption experiment was performed as follows: 5.0 mg of the DBP-FMIPs or DBP-FNIPs were incubated with 7.0 mL 50 % ethanol solution at different concentrations in DBP (5.0–100 mg L−1) for 24 h. After centrifuging at 10000 rpm for 10 min, the DBP concentrations in the supernatants were determined by a UV spectrophotometer. The binding capacity of DBP-FMIPs or DBP-FNIPs for DBP is calculated using the following formula (1):
(1)
Q e
=
(
C i
−
C e
)
V
m
where, Q
e (mg g−1) represents the equilibrium adsorption capacity, C
i (mg L−1) denotes the initial DBP solution concentration, C
e (mg L−1) indicates the equilibrium DBP concentration, V (L) represents the volume of DBP solution, m (g) signifies the mass of DBP-FMIPs or DBP-FNIPs.

The dynamic adsorption experiment was performed as follows: 5.0 mg of the DBP-FMIPs or DBP-FNIPs were mixed with 7.0 mL DBP in 50 % ethanol aqueous solution. Then shake the mixture at room temperature, take it out every 5–100 min, and centrifuge. The concentration of DBP in the supernatant was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 276 nm. The adsorption capacity at different times (t) was calculated using Equation (2).
(2)
Q t
=
(
C 0
−
C t
)
V
m
where, Q
t (mg g−1) represents the adsorption amount at a certain time t, C
0 (100 mg L−1) denotes the initial solution concentration, C
t (mg L−1) signifies the supernatant concentration at different times, V (L) indicates the volume of the DBP solution, and m (g) denotes the mass of DBP-FMIPs or DBP-FNIPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and ratiometric fluorescence detection of DBP
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence detection of DBP: 1.0 mL of DBP 40 % ethanol aqueous solution at various concentrations (0.020–20 mg L−1) was added to 1.0 mL of DBP-FMIPs aqueous solution (0.50 mg mL−1). The mixture was oscillated at room temperature for 3 min. The fluorescence quenching efficiency at 500 nm (F0/F-1) in the absence and presence of DBP was measured to establish a standard curve for the quantitative analysis of DBP.

Ratiometric fluorescence detection of DBP: 1.0 mL of DBP 40 % ethanol aqueous solution at various concentrations (0.020–20 mg L−1) was added to 1.0 mL of DBP-FMIPs aqueous solution (0.50 mg mL−1), followed by the addition of 50 μL of R-CDs. The mixture was oscillated at room temperature for 3 min. The fluorescence spectra of the system were then recorded between 390 and 700 nm using an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. The fluorescence intensity ratio at 500 nm and 680 nm ((I500/I680)0/(I500/I680)) in the absence and presence of DBP was measured to establish a standard curve for the quantitative analysis of DBP.

Selective experiment of DBP-FMIPs: 1.0 mL of DBP, BA, PHP, OPD, DMP, BBP, and DHP 40 % ethanol aqueous solution at concentrations of 2.0 mg L−1 was added to 1.0 mL of DBP-FMIPs aqueous solution (0.50 mg mL−1). After the mixture was oscillated at room temperature for 3 min, its fluorescence intensity at 500 nm was measured using the method described in Section 2.6.

Anti-interference ability of DBP-FMIPs: 1.0 mL of various substances including glucose, urea, common metal ions (Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, and K+), citric acid, sodium citrate, vitamin C, and vitamin B1 of 2.0 mg L−1 were mixed with 1.0 mL of DBP-FMIPs 40 % ethanol aqueous solution (0.50 mg mL−1). The mixture was oscillated at room temperature for 3 min. Its fluorescence intensity at 500 nm was measured using the method described in Section 2.6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DBP in liquor and drinking water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Liquor and drinking water samples were purchased from a local supermarket and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. Subsequently, the supernatant underwent filtration using a 0.45 μm cellulose membrane prior to subsequent analysis [36]. For determination of DBP in liquor samples, 1.0 mL of the liquor sample was mixed with 1.0 mL of DBP-FMIPs aqueous solution and 50 μL of R-CDs aqueous solution. For determination of DBP in drinking water samples, 1.0 mL of the drinking water sample was mixed with 1.0 mL of DBP-FMIPs 40 % ethanol aqueous solution and 50 μL of R-CDs aqueous solution. Shake the mixture at room temperature for 3 min, and measure the fluorescence intensity ratio at 500 nm and 680 nm ((I500/I680)0/(I500/I680)) according to the method described in Section 2.6. All fluorescence tests were conducted a minimum of three times.

Usually, FMIPs are constructed by incorporating fluorescent materials into the MIPs synthesis system [37]. However, this conventional approach not only involves a complex synthesis process but also risks uneven distribution of fluorescent materials within the FMIPs. This uneven distribution can result in weak fluorescence responses, as imprinting sites may be far from the fluorescent materials. In response to this challenge, we have devised a simple and innovative method for producing highly selective FMIPs. This method relies on employing an electron-donor–acceptor type dipolar structure within a compact benzene backbone, TFT, as both crosslinking agent and fluorescent response signal.

The synthesis process of DBP-FMIPs is illustrated in Fig. 1 a. DBP-FMIPs were prepared using DBP-SAM as precursors and TFT as fluorescence crosslinking agent. DBP-SAM, formed through the host-guest interaction between DBP and SAM, exhibits a helical structure characterized by numerous hydroxyl groups on its surface. TFT, acting as a rigid crosslinking agent, undergoes nucleophilic aromatic substitution with the hydroxyl groups on DBP-SAM, thereby fixing the helical structure of DBP-SAM and yielding DBP-FMIPs. Subsequent removal of the template molecule DBP leaves behind imprinted cavities within the DBP-FMIPs' structure, enabling specific recognition for DBP.

The preliminary experimental results suggest that the amount of DBP encapsulated within SAM affects the final selective adsorption capacity of DBP-FMIPs for DBP. Hence, DBP-SAM was prepared with the mass ratios of DBP to SAM ranging from 0.3:5 to 3:5, and the loading capacity of SAM was calculated using formula S1-1. As illustrated in Fig. S1a, it is evident that as the weight ratio of DBP to SAM increases from 0.3:5 to 1:5, the loading capacity of SAM for DBP gradually increases from 7.2 mg g−1 to 19.3 mg g−1. However, further increasing the weight ratio of DBP to SAM does not result in a corresponding increase in the loading capacity of SAM for DBP. Therefore, we selected the complex prepared with a weight ratio of SAM to DBP of 1:5 as the precursor for DBP-FMIPs preparation.

Further experiments reveal that the dosage of TFT plays a pivotal role in influencing the adsorption capacity (Q, mg g−1) and imprinting factor (IF) of DBP-FMIPs. As depicted in Fig. S1b, when the mass ratio of TFT to DBP-SAM increases from 1:5 to 2:5, both Q and IF of DBP-SAM rise concurrently, reaching their peak values at 2:5, namely 24.3 mg g−1 and 6.1, respectively. However, with a continued increase in the mass ratio of TFT to DBP-SAM, both Q and IF gradually decline in unison. These experimental findings suggest that insufficient TFT dosage fails to adequately stabilize the helical structure of DBP-SAM, resulting in lower Q and IF of DBP-FMIPs. Conversely, excessive TFT dosage overly cross-links the helical structure of DBP-SAM, increasing the rigidity of the DBP-FMIPs structure, and thereby hindering the adsorption and desorption of DBP. Ultimately, the TFT to DBP-SAM mass ratio of 2:5 is determined as the optimal preparation condition for DBP-FMIPs.

The preparation and characterization of SAM are elaborated in the supporting information. The average molecular weight of SAM, determined through the light scattering method, is found to be 9010 Da (Fig. S2). Furthermore, XRD test results confirm its characteristic B-type crystalline structure (Fig. S3a).

DBP-SAM was prepared using a precipitation method, known to induce a helical structure, as evidenced by characteristic peaks in XRD at 2θ ≈ 7.8°, 13.6°, and 20.8° (Fig. S3a) [38,39]. The formation of this helical complex was further affirmed by CD (Fig. S3b). Since DBP is an achiral compound, no CD signal is observed. SAM exhibits a relatively weak CD signal at 204 nm. However, upon the formation of the helical complex between DBP and SAM, a prominent absorption peak emerges at 292 nm, providing compelling evidence for the establishment of the helical structure.

Fig. 2 a illustrates the FT-IR spectra of DBP, SAM, DBP-SAM, DBP-FNIPs, and DBP-FMIPs. A discernible weak CO stretching vibration peak at 1730.8 cm−1 is observed in the FT-IR spectrum of DBP-SAM compared to SAM, indicating that DBP has been encapsulated into SAM. Apart from the stretching vibration peak of CN at 2240 cm−1, the FT-IR spectra of DBP-FNIPs and DBP-FMIPs resemble that of SAM [40], suggesting successful cross-linking of DBP-SAM by TFT and suggesting that the backbone structure of DBP-SAM remains unchanged after cross-linking.

XPS was employed to characterize the surface functional groups and elemental composition of DBP-FMIPs. Compared to the XPS spectrum of SAM, the spectrum of DBP-FMIPs exhibited characteristic peaks corresponding to C1s and O1s at 286.3 eV and 533.3 eV, respectively, along with a distinctive peak attributed to F1s at 689.3 eV (Fig. 2b). Additionally, the positions of the three fitted peaks in the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s for SAM (Fig. 2c) and DBP-FMIPs (Fig. 2d) differed. The presence of peaks at 287.8 eV and 285.4 eV in DBP-FMIPs indicated the presence of C–F and C–N groups, absent in SAM, thereby further affirming the successful preparation of DBP-FMIPs.

Fig. 2e displays the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of DBP-FMIPs and DBP-SAM. The BET surface area, average pore diameter, and total pore volume of DBP-FMIPs are 57.85 m2 g−1, 196.4 nm, and 0.28 cm3 g−1, respectively (Fig. S4a). These values are significantly higher than those of DBP-SAM (5.28 m2 g−1, 24.98 nm, and 0.0030 cm3 g−1) (Fig. S4b), while DBP-FNIPs does not have nitrogen adsorption. The above findings indicate that the porosity of DBP-FMIPs originates from its precursor (DBP-SAM).

SEM was utilized to examine the morphology and microstructure of SAM, DBP266 SAM, DBP-FMIPs, and DBP-FNIPs. SAM exhibits large, irregular particles (diameter greater than 10 μm) with a dense structure (Fig. 2f). XRD analysis indicates that it has a B-type crystal structure (Fig. S3a), consistent with findings in related literature [41]. Upon forming a helical complex with DBP, the crystal structure changes from the B-type of SAM to the V-type of DBP-SAM, and the average particle size decreases to 2.2 μm (Fig. 2g and S5a). After TFT cross-linking, DBP-FMIPs (Fig. 2h) exhibit a sheet-like structure composed of small particles (average size of 1.9 μm, Fig. S5b) with a rough and porous surface, and their average particle size is slightly smaller than that of DBP-SAM. In Fig. 2i, DBP-FNIPs show larger particles (average size of 11.4 μm, Fig. S5c), resembling the morphology and size of SAM particles. These findings further indicate that the formation of a helical complex between DBP and SAM is key to achieving porous DBP-FMIPs.

The optical properties of DBP-FMIPs were also examined. As depicted in Fig. S6a, TFT appears as a white powder under natural light and does not exhibit fluorescence under UV light. In contrast, DBP-FMIPs appear as yellow powder under natural light and emit intense yellow-green fluorescence under UV light. Furthermore, ethanol-water solutions (1:1, v/v) containing TFT and DBP-FMIPs display blue and blue-green fluorescence, respectively, under UV light (Fig. S6b-c). TFT is a monobenzene fluorophore characterized by a robust donor-acceptor framework, featuring an electron push-pull system within its structure. The TFT solution displays a maximum emission wavelength of 458 nm (Fig. S6d). Upon replacing the hydroxyl groups on the DBP-SAM surface with TFT nucleophilic aromatic substituents, the electron push-pull system shifts from CN–F to CN-OR, resulting in a red shift of the maximum emission wavelength of TFT in DBP-FMIPs to 500 nm (Fig. S6e). Additionally, both TFT and DBP-FMIPs exhibit excitation independence within the range of 350–400 nm. The fluorescence intensity of DBP-FMIPs remains nearly constant across the pH range of 4–11 (Fig. S6f), and it also demonstrates stability under both natural and ultraviolet light conditions (Fig. S6g and h). These attributes underscore the substantial practical value of DBP-FMIPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption properties of DBP-FMIPs and DBP-FNIPs for DBP
ce:section.ce:para: The isothermal adsorption curves of DBP-FMIPs and DBP-FNIPs were illustrated in Fig. S7. The binding amount of DBP-MIPs or DBP-FNIPs to DBP increases with the initial concentration of DBP. Obviously, the increase rate of DBP-FMIPs is faster and higher than that of DBP-FNIPs throughout the entire experiment concentration, mainly due to the larger specific surface area and more pore structure of DBP-FMIPs compared to DBP-FNIPs. The adsorption capacity of DBP-FMIPs was ultimately determined to be 24.8 mg g−1, exceeding the adsorption capacity of DBP-FNIPs (4.3 mg g−1). The langmuir and freundlich models were used to fit the adsorption process of DBP-FMIPs on DBP (Table S1 and Fig. S7b-c). The calculated maximum adsorption capacity of the langmuir model (25.3 mg g−1) was close to the measured experimental value with higher correlation coefficient. These results indicated that the adsorption of DBF-FMIPs could be better fitted by the langmuir model. Specific information can be found in the supporting information.

The mass transfer characteristic of DBP-FMIPs or DBP-FNIPs was evaluated using dynamic adsorption experiments (Fig. S8). The adsorption rate of DBP-FMIPs is faster than that of DBP-FNIPs, indicating that the porous structure and larger specific surface area of DBP-FMIPs are conducive to rapid mass transfer. The first-order kinetic model and the second-order kinetic model were used to fit the data. Table S2 listed the relevant parameters and R2 of the two models. According to the R2, the adsorptions of DBP-FMIPs and DBP-FNIPs conformed to the second-order kinetic model, so the adsorption type belonged to chemical adsorption.

The reusability of DBP-MIPs was also investigated (Fig. S9). After five cycles of reuse, the adsorption capacity of DBP-FMIPs decreased by only 10 %, indicating that this material has excellent reusability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the ratiometric fluorescence probe for DBP detection
ce:section.ce:para: Utilizing the fact that DBP can quench the fluorescence of DBP-FMIPs (serving as the response signal) while leaving the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs (serving as an internal reference signal) unaffected, we developed a ratiometric fluorescence probe (R-CDs/DBP-FMIPs) for the sensitive detection of DBP. The underlying principle of the R-CDs/DBP-FMIPs for DBP quantification is illustrated in Fig. 1b. Upon the addition of DBP into the probe and excitation at 370 nm, the blue-green emission of DBP-FMIPs at 500 nm undergoes a significant decrease, indicating its suitability as a signal indicator. In contrast, the red emission of R-CDs at 680 nm remains nearly unchanged, rendering it an ideal reference signal. To ensure optimal probe performance, crucial factors affecting the measurements were meticulously optimized, including the concentration of DBP-FMIPs, pH, incubation time, and dosage of R-CDs. The experimental results are depicted in Figs. S10a–d.

The influence of solution pH on the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F-1) of DBP-FMIPs by DBP was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S10a, it is evident that within the pH range of 4–11, pH exerts a relatively minor effect on the (F0/F-1), peaking at pH 7. Beyond pH 11, the (F0/F-1) decreases significantly. This experimental finding also suggests that the interaction between DBP and DBP-FMIPs is predominantly hydrophobic rather than governed by hydrogen bonding. Moreover, this underscores that when employing DBP-FMIPs for DBP detection, the system remains relatively insensitive to variations in pH across a broad range of solution pH (4–11).

The concentration of DBP-FMIPs notably affects the (F0/F-1). When the concentration of DBP-FMIPs increases from 0.30 to 0.50 mg mL−1, there is no significant alteration in the (F0/F-1). However, with further increases in the concentration of DBP-FMIPs (≥0.5 mg mL−1), the (F0/F-1) exhibits a noticeable decline (Fig. S10b). To ensure a more pronounced fluorescence color change in the ratiometric fluorescence probe constructed with DBP-FMIPs and R-CDs for detecting DBP, we determined the optimal concentration of DBP-FMIPs to be 0.50 mg mL−1 in this experiment.

The incubation time between DBP and DBP-FMIPs was further explored (Fig. S10c). The findings reveal that upon the addition of DBP, the fluorescence intensity of DBP-FMIPs steadily decreases with time, stabilizing after 2 min, showcasing the prompt responsiveness of DBP-FMIPs to DBP. To ensure adequate reaction between DBP and DBP-FMIPs, 3 min was selected as the incubation time in the experiment.

The dosage of R-CDs not only affects the sensitivity of R-CDs/DBP-FMIPs in detecting DBP but also influences the extent of color change of the probe during the detection process. As displayed in Fig. S10d, when the dosage of R-CDs is 50 μL, R-CDs/DBP-FMIPs demonstrate the highest sensitivity to DBP detection, accompanied by the most prominent color change of the probe, transitioning from blue to light purple to pink, and finally to red (Fig. 1b). Therefore, 50 μL was chosen as the optimal dosage of R-CDs in the experiment.
In conclusion, the experimental conditions for detecting DBP using R-CDs/DBP-FMIPs are as follows: pH value of 7, DBP-FMIPs concentration of 500 mg L−1, incubation time of 3 min, and 50 μL of R-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability of DBP-FMIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity of DBP-FMIPs: Four phthalate compounds structurally similar to DBP — dimethyl phthalate (DMP), butyl benzyl phthalate (BBP), potassium hydrogen phthalate (PHP), and dihexyl phthalate (DHP) — were selected to evaluate the selectivity of DBP-FMIPs. Their chemical structures are depicted in Fig. S11. From Fig. 3 a, it is evident that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of DBP towards DBP-FMIPs is notably superior to that of the other four structurally similar phthalates. This disparity can be attributed to the highly complementary nature of the imprinted cavities of DBP-FMIPs to DBP in terms of binding site, size, and shape. Additionally, DMP exhibits a higher fluorescence quenching efficiency towards DBP-FMIPs compared to DHP, PHP, and BBP. This distinction may be attributed to the smaller size of DMP relative to DBP, allowing it to penetrate the imprinting cavity of DBP-FMIPs and consequently induce a certain degree of fluorescence quenching. While PHP's size is smaller than that of DBP, its chemical properties diverge from DBP, displaying pronounced hydrophilicity. This property diminishes its likelihood to infiltrate the hydrophobic cavity of DBP-FMIPs, thereby yielding lower fluorescence quenching efficiency compared to DMP. Conversely, the larger size of DHP and BBP relative to DBP poses challenges in entering the imprinting cavity of DBP-FMIPs, consequently resulting in diminished fluorescence quenching efficiency.

The selection of BA and OPD as non-structural analogs of DBP further examined the selectivity of DBP-FMIPs. The results indicate that both BA and OPD exhibit relatively low quenching efficiency towards DBP-FMIPs. From Fig. 3a, it can also be observed that all compounds exhibit very low quenching efficiency towards DBP-FNIPs, further confirming that the selectivity of DBP-FMIPs arises from the helical structure present internally.

Anti-interference ability of DBP-FMIPs: The anti-interference ability of DBP-FMIPs was also examined. The selected potential substances included glucose, urea, common metal ions (Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, and K+), citric acid, sodium citrate, vitamin C, and vitamin B1. As shown in Fig. 3b, the influence of these potential substances on the quenching efficiency is almost negligible, indicating that DBP-FMIPs possess excellent anti-interference capability and promising potential for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of ratiometric fluorescence method for detection DBP
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimal conditions outlined above, the fluorescence signal of R-CDs/DBP-FMIPs at 500 nm decreased as DBP concentrations increased, while the fluorescence signal at 680 nm remained constant (Fig. 4 a). The fluorescence intensity ratio ((I500/I680)0/(I500/I680)) exhibited a linear correlation with DBP concentration, represented by the equation y = 0.07007x +1.02808 (R
2 = 0.996), spanning a linear range of 0.020–20.0 mg L−1 (Fig. 4b). The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 4.5 μg L−1 based on 3σ/K. When using only DBP-FMIPs as the probe, the F
0
/F-1 increased linearly with DBP concentration (Fig. 4d), with a linear range of 0.020–10 mg L−1 (y = 0.05758x +0.05005, R
2 = 0.998) and an LOD of 5.1 μg L−1.

The incorporation of DBP-FMIPs and R-CDs as the ratio fluorescence probe extends the detectable concentration range of DBP compared to using only DBP-FMIPs as the probe, demonstrating promising application prospects. Additionally, as depicted in the inset of Fig. 4a, the color of R-CDs/DBP-FMIPs under 365 nm ultraviolet light shifts notably from blue to red with increasing DBP concentrations. In contrast, the color change of DBP-FMIPs with increasing DBP concentration is less pronounced (Fig. 4c), indicating that the ratiometric fluorescence method developed in this study enables visual DBP detection.

Compared to R-CDs/DBP-FMIPs, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I500/I680) of R-CDs/DBP-FNIPs shows a less significant decrease with increasing DBP concentration, primarily due to the absence of specific recognition sites in DBP-FNIPs (Fig. S12a). The linear equation for R-CDs/DBP-FNIPs sensing probe is y = 0.00535x + 1.09538 (0.020–20 mg L−1), with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.992 (Fig. S12b). This demonstrates that the proposed preparation method for fluorescent MIPs is not only simple but also capable of achieving superior imprinting effects and recognition performance.

The feasibility of the proposed method for detecting DBP in liquor and water actual samples was investigated. No DBP was detected in the samples. To further investigate the method's applicability, spike recovery experiments were conducted on these samples. The spike recoveries of DBP in the five samples ranged from 88 % to 107 %, with RSD below 6.4 %. The analysis results are also consistent with those of traditional HPLC analysis and summarized in Table 1 . Table 2 compares the proposed method with other reported fluorescence detection methods for DBP. The results indicate that this method is comparable to the reported methods in terms of detection limits, recovery rates, and precision, but it has a faster response time. This demonstrates that the ratiometric fluorescence probe based on R-CDs/DBP-FMIPs is suitable for the rapid analysis of DBP in liquor and water samples.

The mechanism behind the fluorescence quenching of DBP-FMIPs by DBP was investigated. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, there is minimal overlap between the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of DBP and the excitation and emission spectra of DBP-FMIPs, thereby eliminating the possibility of an internal filtering effect and energy transfer between DBP and DBP-FMIPs as the quenching mechanism. The fluorescence emission of DBP-FMIPs originates from an electron donor–acceptor type dipolar structure within TFT [47]. When DBP interacts with the imprint cavity of DBP-FMIPs, hindering electron transitions in TFT and consequently leads to the fluorescence quenching of DBP-FMIPs. To substantiate this hypothesis, we conducted confirmation using UV absorption spectroscopy. In Fig. 5b, the UV absorption spectra of DBP and DBP-FMIPs before and after binding are presented. Upon interaction with DBP-FMIPs, the UV absorption peaks of DBP at 229 nm and 280 nm respectively shift to 224 nm and 275 nm with a blue shift of 5 nm, indicating a strong interaction between the two. Additionally, DMP induces fluorescence quenching of DBP-FMIPs to some extent. Fig. 5c illustrates that upon interaction with DBP-FMIPs, the absorption peaks of DMP at 205 nm and 277 nm shift to 203 nm and 275 nm, respectively, with a blue shift of 2 nm, indicating a certain degree of interaction between them, although weaker than with DBP. Conversely, the positions of the absorption peaks of other structural and non-structural analogs (BBP, PHP, DHP, BA, and OPD) remain unchanged after interaction with DBP-FMIPs (Fig. S13), suggesting weaker interactions between them and DBP-FMIPs. These results align closely with the fluorescence quenching efficiencies of these compounds on DBP-FMIPs (Fig. 3a). Furthermore, this underscores the high selectivity of DBP-FMIPs, stemming from the imprint cavities, which exhibit a high degree of complementarity to DBP in terms of binding sites, size, and shape.

Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime of DBP-FMIPs was measured in the presence and absence of DBP to provide further confirmation of this quenching mechanism. As shown in Fig. 5d, upon binding with DBP, the fluorescence lifetime of DBP-FMIPs decreased from 5.5 ns to 4.7 ns, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of DBP-FMIPs is due to DBP hindering the transition of electrons in TFT. The above results underscore the versatility of our proposed method for preparing FMIPs based on TFT, particularly for compounds lacking suitable fluorescent reporting signals.

In this study, we developed a novel method for fabricating fluorescent MIPs (DBP-FMIPs) using TFT, which has an electron-donor-acceptor type dipolar structure within a compact benzene backbone, as both a crosslinking agent and a fluorescent response signal. Additionally, we used short amylose, capable of forming a helical inclusion complex with DBP, as the functional monomer. DBP-FMIPs not only exhibit outstanding fluorescence stability but also exhibit excitation independence and significant Stokes shift. Upon specific binding of DBP to DBP-FMIPs, alterations occur in an electron-donor – acceptor within TFT, resulting in fluorescence quenching. By integrating DBP-FMIPs with red R-CDs, we have developed a ratiometric fluorescence probe (R-CDs/DBP-FMIPs) for detecting DBP in liquor and drinking water. Compared to using DBP-FMIPs alone, R-CDs/DBP-FMIPs not only expand the linear range of DBP detection but also facilitate visual detection of DBP. The method proposed for preparing DBP-FMIPs is simple, convenient, and extendable to the preparation of FMIPs for other organic pollutants capable of forming complexes with amylose, offering a versatile approach for the rapid and selective detection of organic pollutants in food samples.

Xu Guo: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Dandan Yang: Writing – review & editing. Yanhua Chen: Writing – review & editing. Jie Ding: Validation. Lan Ding: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Data curation. Daqian Song: Validation.
